description.s: View code changes now live-reload (rendering system watches
and bypasses selector filtering when the view itself changed). But library changes still require a page refresh — updating a library item doesn't trigger re-renders of views that use it.
Hob views re-evaluate from scratch on each render. Every

call executes again, so if the module cache is invalidated, the next render automatically picks up new library code. In JS, views capture library exports in closures at load time — even after cache invalidation, existing closures still hold old references.
The Hob interpreter controls execution, so

calls during eval can be intercepted to build a dependency graph: "this render instance depends on libraries X, Y, Z." When library X is updated, the system knows exactly which instances to re-render. This is the same pattern as
— the interpreter already tracks which items a render depends on.
registers the library's item ID as a dependency on the current render instance (analogous to how
for a library, look up dependent instances and schedule re-renders
Invalidate the module cache entry for that library so re-eval gets fresh code
Bypass selector filtering for library changes (same as view changes)

Should library dependencies be tracked per-instance (like selectors) or globally? Per-instance is more precise but global may be fine in practice — libraries rarely change, and when they do you probably want everything using them to refresh.

Harder for JS views because closures capture library exports at module load time. Even with cache invalidation, existing JS view code still references old exports. Full re-render (re-evaluating the JS module from scratch) would be needed, which is effectively what Hob already does naturally.
